# monster-guard

Admission, capability **delegation + revocation**, and runtime **enforcement +
leases** for Moonshine plugins. Part of the monster family; the broker behind the
[`.moonshine`](../../.moonshine) plugin registry. Built on the Rust
[`a0n-auth`](../../auth/rust) UCAN crate.

- **What:** a small Rust binary that delegates a UCAN capability per command,
  then runs each invocation under the macOS sandbox (`sandbox-exec`) inside a
  time-boxed, auto-renewing capability lease.
- **Why:** registry caps (`fs,net,exec`) should be real, revocable boundaries —
  and a rogue or revoked process should die, not linger.
- **How:** `setup.sh` `delegate`s each plugin and installs a wrapper that routes
  it through `monster-guard run`. The lease re-checks the revocation store on
  every renewal tick, so `monster-guard revoke` kills running invocations.
- **Verified:** the sandbox + lease + revoke path is exercised end to end by the
  [`monster-swarm`](../monster-swarm) Qwen quality gate, which `delegate`s a grant
  and `run`s a real `fat-station` subagent under an auto-renewing lease while the
  mesh predicts ` Paris` (token 12095) for "The capital of France is". See the
  [`MONSTER_AGENTIC_RUNBOOK`](../MONSTER_AGENTIC_RUNBOOK.md).
- **Next:** the [`monster-resident`](../monster-resident) MCP server is now
  shipped (local fat-client stations get verified orchestration with the UCAN
  programmed in), and the [`monster-swarm`](../monster-swarm) guardrails build on
  this enforcement path. Remaining: per-invocation signatures rather than bearer
  grants.

## Commands

```
monster-guard delegate <name> <caps> [--ttl <secs>]
    Mint a "command grant" UCAN (root -> plugin:<name>, caps as constraints).

monster-guard run --name <name> --caps <caps> [--lease <secs>] [--max <secs>] -- <cmd> [args...]
    Verify the grant (signature + expiry + revocation + caps subset), mint a
    short per-invocation lease, run <cmd> under the caps sandbox, and supervise:
    renew while authorized, kill on expiry / revocation / hard ceiling.

monster-guard revoke command <name> | invocation <jti> | token <token>
    Add to the revocation store. Running invocations die at the next lease tick;
    future runs are denied.

monster-guard role list | show <role>
    List the role registry / dump one role's bundle as JSON.

monster-guard role grant <agent> <role> [--ws <ws>] [--scope <path>] [--ttl <secs>]
                                        [--oath <file>] [--qspec <file>] [--bowl <file>]
    Resolve a role's capability bundle against (workspace, scope) and mint a UCAN
    grant carrying the WHOLE bundle. Stored as grants/<agent>.ucan; runnable with
    `monster-guard run --name <agent>` (caps derived from the bundle).

monster-guard role check <agent> <can> <resource> [--cmd <command>] [--ws <ws>]
    The fine-grained VFS gate: does <agent>'s grant authorize <can> on <resource>
    (and, for exec/run, <command>)? Prints ALLOW / DENY, exits 0 / 2.

monster-guard role define <role> <file.json> --as <admin>
monster-guard role rm <role> --as <admin>
    Add/override or remove a role in the on-disk registry. Self-hosting: gated by
    a `role/admin` grant (--as), so the policy is controlled from inside moonshine.
```

`caps` is a comma list of `fs`, `net`, `exec` (or `-`). Enforcement (when the
target binary is signed, which macOS requires to sandbox):

| missing cap | Seatbelt rule         | effect                      |
| :---------- | :-------------------- | :-------------------------- |
| `net`       | `(deny network*)`     | no network sockets          |
| `fs`        | `(deny file-write*)`  | read-only filesystem        |
| `exec`      | `(deny process-fork)` | cannot fork child processes |

Unsigned binaries can't be sandboxed by macOS; they run unsandboxed (with a
note) but the **lease + revocation still apply**.

## State

Under `$MONSTER_GUARD_HOME` (default `~/.moonshine.d`; `setup.sh` points it at
`open-source/.moonshine.d`):

- `root.key` — root authority secret (hex, `0600`). **Secret; gitignored.**
- `grants/<name>.ucan` — per-command (or per-agent role) delegation token.
- `revocations.json` — `RevocationSet` (token hashes / issuer DIDs / jti).
- `roles/<name>.json` — on-disk role overrides (shadow/extend the built-ins).

## Lease model

A run is time-boxed by the lease (`--lease`, default 60s). The supervisor renews
while the grant verifies and is not revoked; it kills the process group if a
renewal is denied (revoked / grant expired), the lease expires without renewal,
or `--max` is exceeded. Exit codes: `124` lease expired / ceiling, `125` revoked,
`2` admission denied, otherwise the child's own code.

## Quality-gated admission (qspec)

Capability validation answers "is the caller *allowed*?". A **quality spec
(qspec)** answers the orthogonal question "does the artifact *actually work*?" —
and makes the answer an **acceptance criterion** for admission. With a qspec,
`delegate` refuses to mint a grant unless the thing proves it works, exactly
parallel to how a capability must validate. One line: **admission proves the
artifact works** — fake-agent code cannot register.

A qspec is a small JSON file declaring one quality probe:

```json
{
  "probe": "paris-probe-rknot",
  "knot": "~/.edgework/models/qwen2.5-0.5b-instruct.knot",
  "prompt_tokens": [785, 6722, 315, 9625, 374],
  "expect_token": 12095,
  "expect": "Paris"
}
```

- `probe` is optional (default `paris-probe-rknot`); a leading `~` in `knot`
  expands to the home directory; `expect` is a human-readable label and is **not**
  verified — only `expect_token` is.
- Verification runs
  `<probe> --dense <knot> --dense-only --tokens <prompt_tokens csv> --expected <expect_token> --top-k 8`
  and **passes iff the probe exits 0**.

The gate and a standalone verifier:

```
monster-guard delegate <name> <caps> [--ttl <secs>] [--qspec <file>]
    With --qspec, run the declared probe BEFORE minting. On failure, print
    "admission denied: quality spec failed", exit 2, and mint NO grant.
    Without --qspec the behavior is unchanged (back-compatible).

monster-guard qspec verify <file>
    Run the probe and report PASS / FAIL (exit 0 / 2).
```

**Verified:** a correct qspec admits (grant minted); a wrong `expect_token`
denies (no grant written). With the 0.5B knot the probe is ~1.2 s cold / ~2.2 s
warm — cheap enough to gate every admission (the heavy 7B mesh gate is ~123 s).

Full operator walkthrough, the `.moonshine` `quality` column, and the
swarm-side `spawn --qspec` are in the
[`MONSTER_AGENTIC_RUNBOOK`](../MONSTER_AGENTIC_RUNBOOK.md#quality-gated-admission-qspec).

## JIT-dequant-gated admission (τ-bowl / bowlspec)

Where a **qspec** answers "does the model produce the right token?", a **bowlspec**
answers the orthogonal *capability* question: **"can this model be served at
coarse-code cost (Q4/E8/Leech, masked per-op) with a CERTIFIED ≤(1−q) quality-loss
bound — distribution-free, no truth at inference?"** It is the third admission
dimension (quality / conduct / **cheap-and-certified**), and the runtime twin of
the conformal coverage cert (`Gnosis.ConformalCoverageAdmissibility`): a config is
admitted iff the conformal threshold clears half the energy margin (`2·Q_q ≤ γ`),
so its serve-time *lie rate* is certified `≤ 1 − q`. The gate is **miss-not-lie**:
it refuses the ops it cannot bound rather than silently degrade them.

Verification reuses the **existing runtime probe** — the `bowl-probe` binary in
[`distributed-inference`](../distributed-inference), which runs the cell-filling
diagnostic + Tukey-bowl accept-rate (cubic vs E_8) + conformal admission on **real
admitted-knot tiles** (no synthetic data). monster-guard owns the *gate*; the
probe owns the *measurement* (no conformal math is duplicated here), exactly as
the qspec gate shells out to `paris-probe-rknot`.

A bowlspec is a small JSON file:

```json
{
  "probe": "bowl-probe",
  "knot": "~/.edgework/models/qwen2.5-0.5b-instruct.knot",
  "quality_profile": "dev-balanced",
  "matrix_type": "any",
  "q_values": [0.90, 0.95, 0.99],
  "gamma_multiplier": 2.0,
  "min_certified_coverage": 0.99
}
```

- only `knot` is required; `probe` defaults to `bowl-probe`, `quality_profile` to
  `dev-balanced`, `matrix_type` to `any`; a leading `~` expands to `$HOME`.
- `min_certified_coverage` (optional) is a **floor**: admission additionally
  requires every coverage `q ≥ floor` to be admitted at the configured γ.
- Verification runs
  `<probe> --knot <knot> --quality-profile <p> --matrix-type <m> --format json [--q-values …] [--gamma-multiplier …]`,
  parses the report, and **passes iff `admission.admitted == true`** (and the
  floor, if any, is met). The certified coverage column is printed for the ledger.

The gate and a standalone verifier:

```
monster-guard delegate <name> <caps> [--ttl <secs>] [--bowl <file>]
    With --bowl, run the τ-bowl conformal probe BEFORE minting. On failure print
    "admission denied: τ-bowl gate failed", exit 2, and mint NO grant. Composable
    with --qspec and --oath (all gates must pass). Back-compatible when omitted.

monster-guard bowl verify <file>
    Run the probe and report PASS / FAIL plus the certified coverage column
    (admitted, e8_energy_ge_cubic, certified_jit_dequant_at, per-q MASK/recompute).
```

**Verified:** end-to-end on the real admitted `qwen2.5-0.5b-instruct.knot` —
`bowl verify` PASSes with `admitted=true`, `e8_energy_ge_cubic=true`, certified at
`q≥0.90` (`q=0.90:MASK lie≤0.100, q=0.95:MASK lie≤0.050`), ~6.5 s on dev-fast; a
passing `delegate --bowl` mints the grant, while an unreachable
`min_certified_coverage` floor denies admission and writes no grant.

## Oath-bound admission

Where a **qspec** gates *quality* ("does the artifact work?"), an **oath** gates
*conduct* ("how has the artifact sworn to behave?"). They are orthogonal and
parallel. The qspec is run at admission and discarded; the oath is
**cryptographically bound into the minted grant** and travels with the capability
for its whole life.

An oath is a small JSON file (`*.oath.json`) declaring a sworn commitment plus
the bounds it accepts:

```json
{
  "oath": "<commitment>",
  "max_caps": ["net"],
  "ttl_max_secs": 3600,
  "sworn_by": "did:key:..."
}
```

- `oath` — the sworn conduct commitment, in prose.
- `max_caps` — capability ceiling; requested `caps` must be a **subset**.
- `ttl_max_secs` — maximum permitted grant lifetime; a longer `--ttl` is denied.
- `sworn_by` — optional DID of the swearer (audit / attribution).

The gate and a standalone verifier:

```
monster-guard delegate <name> <caps> [--ttl <secs>] [--qspec <file>] [--oath <file>]
    With --oath, BEFORE minting check the request against the oath's bounds and
    DENY admission if:
      - requested caps are NOT a subset of max_caps, or
      - requested ttl exceeds ttl_max_secs.
    On success, BIND the oath into the minted grant as the UCAN `fct` — so the
    declared conduct contract is signed into the capability and travels with it.
    Without --oath the behavior is unchanged (back-compatible).

monster-guard oath verify <file>
    Validate the oathspec and report PASS / FAIL.
```

The binding rides a0n-auth's `IssueOptions.facts`, which is what carries the
`fct` field of the issued UCAN. Downstream, a conduct monitor (e.g. the
monster-badchild rogue-bodyguard layer) reads the bound oath as the contract it
enforces against at runtime; Thoth's Oath gate (`ThothGateScore::Oath`) scores
it. Reference oaths live at
[`../monster-swarm/oaths/`](../monster-swarm/oaths). The swarm-side `spawn --oath`
(ledger + fleet frame) and the user-spawnable flow are in the
[`MONSTER_AGENTIC_RUNBOOK`](../MONSTER_AGENTIC_RUNBOOK.md#oaths-and-user-spawnable-agents).

### Signed oath-tokens (cryptographic proof the swearer swore it)

Raw JSON proves the *bounds*, but not that the **swearer** swore them — a raw
oath is text bound into the grant `fct`, signed only by ROOT (the admitter).
`delegate --oath <file>` now also accepts a **signed oath-token**: a UCAN minted
by the swearer that carries the oath in its own `fct`, so the contract carries
the swearer's signature, not just root's attestation.

The token format:

```
iss = <swearer DID>            # who swears it (signs the token)
aud = <agent DID>             # who is bound (iss == aud is a Ulysses self-bind)
att = [{ can: "oath/uphold", with: "agent:self:<id>" }]
fct = { oath, max_caps, ttl_max_secs?, sworn_by? }
<signed by the swearer's key>
```

`delegate --oath` detects the form and behaves accordingly:

- **Signed token:** `verify_chain` the signature — **deny** on invalid, forged,
  or expired (no grant minted). Require the `oath/uphold` capability. Read the
  terms from `fct` and run the same caps-subset + ttl checks against those
  *verified* terms. On success, bind the verified terms **plus proof metadata**
  `{ sworn_by, ulysses, signed }` into the grant `fct`, so the crypto provenance
  travels with the capability.
- **Raw JSON:** unchanged, back-compatible — bound as before and marked
  **unsigned** (`signed: false`).

A **Ulysses** token (`iss == aud`) is a self-issued, self-audienced irrevocable
precommitment — the agent ties itself to the mast. A **co-signed** oath is a
2-link chain handed down via `IssueOptions.proofs` (the spawner imposes, the
agent accepts), each party's signature recorded in the chain. `oath verify`
reports `signed` / `swearer` / `ulysses` for a token. No a0n-auth change was
needed: `IssueOptions.facts` carries the `fct`, `IssueOptions.proofs` the prf
chain, and `verify_chain` validates the token.

### Registry double gate (quality AND oath)

Admission can now require **both** gates together. The
[`.moonshine`](../../.moonshine) registry carries a **`quality`** column and an
**`oath`** column (7 columns total: `name kind path caps build quality oath`),
and `setup.sh` passes each to `delegate` as `--qspec <quality>` / `--oath
<oath>`. A registered command is therefore admitted **only if its quality probe
passes AND its oath verifies** (caps within the oath, ttl within bound, signature
valid for a signed token) — capability, budget, *works*, and *behaves*, all
checked before a grant exists. A `-` in either column skips that gate
(back-compatible). The full registry walkthrough and a worked example are in the
[`MONSTER_AGENTIC_RUNBOOK`](../MONSTER_AGENTIC_RUNBOOK.md#admission-registry-quality--oath).

## Roles — bundled VFS policy

A **role** is a reusable, named bundle of fine-grained VFS capabilities — the unit
you actually hand to an agent. Where an `oath` gates *conduct* and a `qspec` gates
*quality*, a role is the *grant shape*: a set of abilities over a path **scope**
within a **workspace**. `role grant` mints a UCAN whose `att` carries the whole
bundle; the moonshine spawn path (or `role check`) gates each request against it.

### Vocabulary

Fine-grained actions (`can`) and the resources (`with`) they apply to:

| `can`         | OS cap | resource                | meaning                          |
| :------------ | :----- | :---------------------- | :------------------------------- |
| `fs/read`     | —      | `vfs:<ws>:<path>`       | read files (reads always OS-OK)  |
| `fs/write`    | `fs`   | `vfs:<ws>:<path>`       | write files                      |
| `exec/run`    | `exec` | `vfs:<ws>:__exec`       | run a command (optional patterns)|
| `net/connect` | `net`  | `vfs:<ws>:__net`        | open the network                 |
| `role/admin`  | —      | `policy:roles`          | mutate the role registry         |

Path scope rides `a0n-auth`'s prefix-wildcard matcher, so granularity is just the
resolved resource string:

| granularity | resource          | example role                         |
| :---------- | :---------------- | :----------------------------------- |
| single file | `vfs:mono:a.ts`   | `developer` (`--scope a.ts`)         |
| directory   | `vfs:mono:src/*`  | `senior-developer` (`--scope src`)   |
| whole tree  | `vfs:mono:*`      | `architect` / `owner`                |

### Two enforcement layers (both honest)

1. **Fine** — the grant's `att` is the per-path bundle; [`role::authorize`] (and
   `role check`) decide a specific request. This is the moonshine VFS gate.
2. **Coarse** — `monster-guard run` derives the union of the bundle's `fs`/`net`/
   `exec` caps and builds the same `sandbox-exec` profile as a plain grant.

### Built-in org chart

`role list` shows the shipped bundles — a private-company org chart:

- **Ownership / admin:** `owner`, `architect` (whole tree rw+exec+net **+ registry admin**).
- **IC ladder:** `staff-engineer`, `senior-developer` (per-dir write), `developer`
  (per-file write — the default), `contractor`, `intern`, `guest` (scoped read).
- **Quality:** `qa-engineer` (read+run tests, **no write**), `sdet` (writes tests).
- **Ops:** `devops-sre`, `release-manager`, `bot-ci`. **Security:** `security-auditor`.
- **Data:** `data-engineer`, `ml-engineer`.
- **Product/design/docs:** `product-manager` (docs only), `designer` (assets only).
- **Read-only oversight:** `engineering-manager`, `analyst`, `researcher`,
  `reviewer`, `auditor`.

### Self-hosting registry (control from inside moonshine)

Built-ins ship in code; overrides live at `$MONSTER_GUARD_HOME/roles/<name>.json`
— inside moonshine. `role define` / `role rm` mutate that directory and are gated
by [`role::require_admin`]: the caller's `--as <admin>` grant must carry
`role/admin` on `policy:roles` (the `owner` / `architect` bundles do). An override
shadows a built-in of the same name; removing it restores the built-in. Bootstrap
the first admin with `role grant <agent> owner` (a root action, like `delegate`).

```
# org owner bootstraps an admin, who then edits policy from inside moonshine:
monster-guard role grant cto architect
monster-guard role define reviewer-bot ./reviewer-bot.json --as cto

# grant a scoped IC role and gate a request:
monster-guard role grant alice developer --scope packages/api/server.ts
monster-guard role check alice fs/write packages/api/server.ts   # ALLOW
monster-guard role check alice fs/write packages/web/app.ts      # DENY
monster-guard role check alice exec/run __exec --cmd vitest      # ALLOW
monster-guard role check alice exec/run __exec --cmd rm          # DENY
```

## Build

```
cargo test          # lease + qspec + bowl + oath + role tests
cargo build --release
```
